sentences.text: I am Rachel Hanan.
My maiden name is Rachel Hougnou, and I came from Rhodes Island, an island, very beautiful, in the Dodecanese.
H-O-U-G-N-O-U.
Hanan, H-A-N-A-N.
I was born Rhodes Island, now called the Island of Rhodes, on the 22nd of October 1924.
My age now is 71.
My father's name was Isaac Hougnou and my mother's name was Perla, maiden, Arlhadeff.
Hougnou, H-O-U-G-N-O-U.
And my mother, P-E-R-L-A, Perla.
Arlhadeff, A-R-L-H-A-D-E-F-F.
My mother was looking after the children because we were eight in the family and she had no time to work outside.
But my father was a tailor, a very good one and very well renowned in the island.
I had four brothers and three sisters, two sisters and myself, three girls and four boys.
My eldest brother was named Joseph, and then came Perahia, P-E-R-A-H-I-A.
And then, Diamante, my sister, my eldest sister.
And then it was Aaron, Jack, and [?
Fortunae ?] and myself.
Very much so.
We were very lucky family.
We were doing everything together, laughing together, crying together, and fighting together.
Of course we had, you know, in that time, you know, it was the fashion, to have-- it was popular to have everybody, you know, all the families, they had 10 children, 9 children, whatever.
So we had just relationship with the-- the sisters of my mother, but not with all of the family because otherwise, it was going to be like 100 cousins, and we had no time to-- to see each other.
But only on festival or Rosh Hashanah and Pesach, we used to visit each other and have the meals together.
And it was like big tables together.
Each one, you know, they prepared their own meal, but we came to a house where there was room for everybody, and we were just having the meals together.
We were.
Everybody on Rhodes Island was Jewish.
I mean, they were very religious.
We used to go-- you know, in Rhodes Island, there used to be two major synagogue.
One, it was called the Kahale Shalom, and the other one was called the Kahal Grande, which, it means in English, the [INAUDIBLE] Shul, the [INAUDIBLE] Shul.
And all of the Jewish people lived on Rhodes Island belonged to one or the other one.
So we belonged to Shalom, Kahal Shalom.
The rabbi at the time when I-- when I was, it was Mr.
Vital ?] that he later immigrate to Israel and later-- and later on in life it was a rabbi called Albagli, A-L-B-A-G-L-I.
Well, we used to have all day Jewish ritual.
First of all, we used to put, you know, the meat and all this, in, you know, in salt and all this like [INAUDIBLE].
And then every Friday night, we used to have, you know, the nice meal.
And nobody was allowed to touch electricity or to open a-- you know, a stove or whatever.
And the-- the festival, let's just say, everybody, you know, was not working on Saturday, on Shabbat.
All day, Jewish-- the Jewish shops were closed on Shabbat.
And you can see it was like a day of-- of Sabbath because the whole commerce in Rhodes Island was done by the Jewish people.
And then, when they closed on Saturday, it was dead.
Nobody was in the-- in the shops because nobody went to shop any more because it was not worth it.
The few more shops, they were from Greeks or other ones, it was not worth visiting.
Like Italian people and Turkish people and Greek people who live in the island never came into the Jewish quarter to shop because there was no-- no point.
It was all the shops were closed.
Yeah, at the certain point, when the prosperity, you know, they were prospering in the community, they had 5,000 Jews.
But after, you know, they started having, you know, the martial laws and this and that.
So people, they were not feeling secure any more in Rhodes Island, and they tried to immigrate.
People went to South America, to Africa, and even to Palestine.
My address was 16 Via Heredia, H-E-R-E-D-I-A, which it was named after Grand Maestro, which it was a Cavalier.
Because Rhodes Island was occupied of the century sometimes by the Turkish, by the Ottoman Empire, for sometimes by the Arabs.
But and at one time the most, you know, the Cavaliers, that they were like the knights, you know, the Knights of St.
John's.
They were occupying Rhodes Island and then many, many streets were named after their names.
So Via Heredia was the name of one the big Cavaliers or whatever you call it.
Well, it was-- you know, in Rhodes Island, there were schools for Jewish people, schools for Greeks, schools for Turkish, and schools for Italians.
So each one, you know, attend to his own school.

So ourselves, we went to the Jewish school and then it was, you know, the-- the fashion when you finish the school, which it was standards in high school, like three or four high school classes, and that's it.

But myself, personally, I attended the Jewish school like everybody else, but at the end of finishing my school, I-- I went to the nuns because I want to pursue my school, my-- my education and go to university.
And there were-- at the time, there were very few who wanted to-- to go to university, but I was one of them.
Very much, my best friends at school and they all liked me.
Because in-- in-- in Rhodes Island, at the time, you know, with Italians because there were Italian schools, nobody was interested in what religion you were.
You were free, freedom of the-- of the-- you know, of the-- of the religion.
Whatever religion you want to do, it was free to do.
And they were not interested because they were good people.
The Italian in general, they were nice, kind people.
What language did you speak at home?
At home, we-- we spoke Spanish because my family is Sephardi and we all came from, you know, descendant from the Spanish Inquisition, you know?
They were all gone to-- some people, you know, after, you know, the Inquisition, people went to Turkey, some to Rhodes Island, some-- and we belong to the Sephardi branch of the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How did you feel about being Jewish at this time?
I was very proud because, as a matter fact, we had a teacher at school, now a Jewish school, that he was one of the chief of revisionism that started the movement in Rhodes Island.
And thus, he'd been our teacher, and he started, you know, teaching as he was a man-- as he was a man.
And later on, he-- he came to-- to North Africa and I mean, and he was the one who-- who help him, Menachem Begin, when he was there in hiding, to escape.
No, well, there-- I mean, we were very happy and it was a very-- you know, Rhodes Island was like a paradise.
You know, even now, after the war, they go plenty-- thousands and thousands of tourists because it's so beautiful.
So much so, you know, they made even a perfume in-- in France, which is called the [?
Notte ?] de [?
Rhodi, ?] in Italian means the-- the-- the nights of Rhodes because it's a smell, it's a perfume that you cannot describe, you know, only in the Orient.
As living, you know, there, and we can-- when you were at night, you know, in summer, especially, and you cannot-- you cannot even sleep, so much perfume, it was all over.
And it was a really very, very happy life we had.

Though we were not very rich, the majority, but everybody was happy because they make their living and they were happy and you could hear in the street, sing-- songs and everybody was singing and you know, they were very, very happy.
What was my what?
Well, I was seeing myself a doctor because I wanted to go to university and I want to study and I want to find some-- some like you say, some medicine, some cure for some diseases unknown.
You know, that's 50 years ago.
You know, it was-- a woman, nowadays, they have discovered many things.
But at the time, it was many-- you know, even for a simple flu, there was nothing that you can have, not even an aspirin or whatever.
So I was trying, I was thinking I will do something for the humanity.
Nothing at all.
We were not aware of what's happening in Europe.
We were not aware of the program because you know, half of the-- of the Jewish population were-- you know, had a-- a radio to listen to.
And we were just reading, just the local newspaper.
They never say anything about those things.

The only thing we-- we heard vaguely, through the Jewish movement you know, that we have-- we were receiving from Italy, that the-- the German was in power, you know, like Hitler, and he was a bad man, and we shouldn't buy products from Germany.
So when we used to go to the-- when we were at school, and we used to go and buy pencils and-- and paper and all this, we were avoiding, like, things made in Germany.
Like Faber, for-- for instance, it was our-- our pencil that we used to do our school.
It was always Faber, the name.
But since we heard those things, you know, bad about him, we avoid buying those products from Germany.
That is the only thing.
Otherwise, life was going on.
The-- the-- the-- the-- the Italians were very friendly as usual.
There was no incident.
There were lots of intermarriages, assimilation.
That means the Italians, as a matter of fact, fancy more the Jewish girl than the other one, but it was the religious beliefs that kept her, you know, to assimilating more.
It was to start with, you know, not about anti-Semitism or whatever.
Our life start to change when the war started.
Because we-- we start feeling the-- the restrictions of buying the food and the clothes and everything.
And we start having, you know, coupons for everything and then that's how our lives started changing a little bit.
And they start the black market.

And then little by little, you know, as I told you before, the Jewish population in Rhodes Island, they were not very rich, and they were just-- just receiving to-- like aid from their own children that were in America, in Africa, and everything.
So when the war start, then they didn't send it anymore because the posts were not coming anymore, and they were just starting to feel like starvation and all this.
They couldn't buy, especially with those black market price.
So that's when the Jewish population in Rhodes Island started, you know, to feel uncomfortable.
Were there any Nazi or fascist groups in your town that-- that you lived?
Were there what?
No.
There were fascists, but we all belong.
Myself, I was a small Italian [INAUDIBLE] so was all the Jewish-- were all the Jewish girl, the Jewish boy because it was like fun.
It was like going every Sunday, to, you know, like you go to seminar.
We were going there to sing songs and everything, and it was fun to be with the other one, with the Italian girls, Italian boys, and it was like nothing.
It was-- we did-- because actually, we like Italy, up to then, you know, we were treated like every other Italian, Jews or non-Jews.
At the beginning, you know, Mussolini was very much lenient towards the-- the-- the Jews.
As a matter of fact, even in Italy, he was-- they use to call him that he was a friend of the Jews.

And in many-- in many speeches that he made, he say, anti-Semitism, it doesn't exist in Italy, and because we are all Italians, and we are-- but after, you know, this, he start having, you know, friendship with Hitler and all this, everything start to change.
And the first thing they-- they start making, you know, these martial laws.
And it was terrible because we didn't expect it, and we didn't even dream to have those treatments.
When, you know, Hitler and Mussolini become aligned, you see?
And the first thing it was, like, in 1938.
In 1938, we were just out of the blue, as I say, not many of them-- of course, we had a radio to listen to.

And then we opened the newspaper, but we-- we used to have the newspaper every day, and the-- the-- the first thing we saw into the newspaper, there were like the face of a Jew with distorted nose, distorted eyes.
Because the people, they were trying now to show that the Jews, they were ugly and like, like Satans or whatever.
And they start-- you know, and-- and we read all the laws about-- about the Jewish.
First off, you know, there were many laws, like you say.

For instance-- the-- the first thing, for instance, all the people, all the soldiers, all the soldiers in the army, even the captains were-- and people that were-- that were, you know, served their country in Italy-- not in Rhodes Island, we are not so many, in Italy.
They were sent away.
They were not allowed to serve in the Army.
And then, you know, the Jews, they were not allowed to close their shops on Saturday.
They-- they were not allowed to observe the festival.
They were not allowed to go to to-- to schools that they were not run by Jewish.
And the Jewish school itself could have only five classes, which is just the first five classes.
And they want us-- to humiliate us and not to-- to let us have, you know, education and to be inferior.

Because in Italy, though we-- there were only all together in the whole peninsula, 40,000 Jews, but they occupied the most prominent places in the government, in science, in the university, all over the places.
They were very well regarded.

And then they they expelled all these people after these martial laws and even the Italians had much difficulty in-- in-- in in-- in find other people to take their-- their jobs because they were really very, very clever people.
My life personally was affected because I was-- I couldn't go anymore to school and couldn't pursue my-- my studies.
That's the-- and I was very, very, very upset.
I was crying day and night because I saw myself finish and then eventually-- eventually, well, I say I have to do something.
I went to the schools where I was in high school, in the [INAUDIBLE] And then there were-- the Mother Superior, they were all nuns, they say, don't worry.
It will be just a matter of few-- of few months and everything will be to normal and you can come back to us because they were very understanding and very loving sisters.
Not at all.
Not at all.
So after that, you know, I went to find, to seek a job because I was not meant to stay at home and cook and bake or whatever.
I want to-- to stay, you know, with the people, you know, that were in an office.
So I-- I-- I-- I find a job, like a bookkeeper, you know?
I was trained.
[INAUDIBLE] a bookkeeper.
And all the girls in the-- in the office, they become very friendly.
They were all Italian girls.
There were about eight or 10.
They become very friendly with me, though, you know, they knew I was Jewish.
After a while, you know, because this office where I was working, it was Institute Nationale de [INAUDIBLE].
It was a government-- a government office run, you know-- the men, you know, branch, it was in Rome.
So they send a letter and they say, even in Rhodes Island, you are not allowed to employ Jewish people.
So when this order come to my branch in-- in Rhodes, the-- the-- the-- manager called me in his office and he told me, sorry.
Though we are very satisfied with your work, but we cannot keep you anymore in our-- in our office because you are Jewish-- you are a Jewish girl and we cannot.
We are not allowed.
You have to leave.
So I have to leave, and I went.
But after a month or two, the girls where I was working with, they came to my house.
They were my friends, and they come and told me, you must come tomorrow to start work all over again.
What's the outcome?
I was not allowed.
They gave an ultimatum, the 10 girls.
If you are going to-- you are not going to take back Rachel, we are all to-- to give, you know, to have to resign.
All and they were all Italian girls.
So the next day, the-- the manager called me in his office, say, you can start working, but the only thing we are not going to tell Rome that you-- we've got a Jewish girl.
They were paying me my salary in cash and nobody knew I was working there.
And after a while, those girls, one by one, they all left.
They went to Italy because the bombardment and the situation in Rhodes Island become very critical.
So later on, I become the manager myself, you see?
But no, in-- in Italy, they didn't know I was there and I was Jewish.
You mentioned the bombardments.
Can you explain what you mean by that?
It means that, you know, we live in this island, Rhodes Island, which is the Juderia, which is the Jewish quarters that you call the ghetto in Poland.
But we were called the Juderia.
Everybody wants to live in the Juderia.
We were not obliged to be there.
We can go and live anywhere or wherever the center is, but we did.
Jews want to be next to the Jews.
We were just in that place.
It happened that the Juderia was the nearest-- the nearest part of the island next to the port.
So now, during the war, there were lots of ships and all these, you know, like German ships, Italian ships in the port were bringing and taking out the soldiers, you know, there.
Imagine that during the time, you know, the population of Rhodes Island, it consist of, like, 40,000.
But during the war, we had 90,000 to 100,000 soldiers, all the Germans and Italians.
So there were a lot of traffic and lots of thing in the port.
So the Allies were trying, you know, to-- to put down all the ships, and they were coming to, you know, to bombard them at night or during the day.
But as, you know, a millimeter of-- of mistake in the air, you know, the bomb, instead-- many of them instead of falling on the-- on the ship, they were falling into the Juderia.
It means that many people, Jewish people were killed.
And the first one of this bombardment happened on Yom Purim and everybody was in shul and the whole half of the Juderia was destroyed.

So after that-- after that, so fearing for our lives, we went and live in the villages because the villages are far away from the port and everybody tried, you know, to hire a little house or to rent a little room or whatever it is, and we went there.
So that's why we were away, you know, from Rhodes Island when the German came later on, to find us.
Did any people try to leave Rhodes Island, Jewish people?
Yes, that is between '38 and '44, the time when we left the island.
So first of all they were trying to go by their own means, you know, with boats and whatever they can find, you know, to go to Turkey.
Other ones, they were even hiding in small boats owned by Turkish and Greek people to go to Anatolia, which is the nearest part of Turkey, nearest part to-- to Rhodes Island.
So and-- and then it happened also in 1940, a big boat carrying about 1,500 refugees from Eastern Europe, they were leaving for Israel.
So it happened that when they were in the vicinity of the waters of Rhodes Island, it burned.
It was, you know, there were a fire.

So the nearest place where this, you know, the captain, he asked the permission to the Italian people, and they came, all the Jewish people to stay in the island until the ship was-- it was fixed or they-- they send another ship for it or something like that.
So when this ship was sent to collect all these people, many of our-- of our young people, they joined them.
They-- when they arrived, when they arrived, you know, there were 1,500 refugees, you know, from Europe.
When they left, there were more than 2,500 of our-- of our-- of our people joined them to go to Palestine.
Did your family ever try to leave?
No.
Because-- because we were little bit well off, you see, better off than many families.
We never-- for instance, during the war, everybody was starving and this.
As-- as I say, we had a little bit-- our condition economically was a little bit above, and we had money to survive even with a black market.
We thought that the war was going to finish.
We never thought that.
If we knew that was happening, it was in store, we were going to go even by, you know, swimming to wherever.
No, except my sister that she survived with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The governor was at the time, you know, it was De Vecchi.
De Vecchi, you know, I don't know if you know the history.
He was one of the-- the quadrumvirate.
You know, when Mussolini formed his government to go, they called the [INAUDIBLE] a Roma It means they were marching to Rome and to conquer, you know, Italy, he was one of the four people.
We think there were three other ones, Mussolini and three other ones.
One of them was De Vecchi.

And De Vecchi, you know, when he come to Rhodes Island to-- to take, you know, the place of our old governor, which was Mario Lago, everything start changing because he was a maniac like all the-- the fascists were.
And he start first of all, when he was going into the street, everybody has to stand wherever they are and salute him.
When you pass an ambulance, for instance, and you have to remain where you are.
He-- he wanted that from, and he start having all these laws, you know?
He was very happy about these things.
So that's why, that he was over in-- in Rhodes Island.
Did any non-Jews try to help the Jews in Rhodes Island?
When we were deported?
When-- when these bombs, then, started to-- to fall?
No, we were-- we didn't-- I mean, what can they help?
But they were just helping as you say, all the Greeks, they-- they offered their houses, though we were paying.
We were paying their rent but they-- they help us.
